title-group.article-title: Utilization of resting-state electroencephalography spectral power in convolutional neural networks for classification of primary progressive aphasia

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Communication Sciences and Disorders, University of Houston, 3871 Holman St, Houston, TX, 77204, USA
bDepartment of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Houston, 4226 Martin Luther King Boulevard, Houston, TX, 77204, USA
cGeriatric Research, Education, and Clinical Center, VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System, University Drive C, Pittsburgh, PA, 15240, USA
dDepartment of Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences, The University of Texas at Austin, 2504a Whitis Ave a1100, Austin, TX, 78712, USA
eDepartment of Neurology, The University of Texas at Austin Dell Medical School, 1501 Red River St, Austin, TX, 78712, USA

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Department of Communication Sciences and Disorders, University of Houston, 3871 Holman St, Houston, TX, 77204, USA. hrdial@central.uh.edu

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: We investigated relative power spectral density (PSD) in primary progressive aphasia (PPA) in delta, theta, alpha, and beta frequency bands in eyes open and closed resting-state electroencephalography (EEG). Our aims were to assess whether discernible differences could be observed between each PPA variant and to determine the utility of PSD for PPA classification when used as input to a convolutional neural network (CNN). Findings in the current study were similar to previous studies in logopenic PPA, with a significant increase in relative PSD in delta and theta bands and a significant reduction in the beta band (consistent with oscillatory slowing). We did not observe a significant increase in power for lower frequency bands or a reduction of power in higher frequency bands for semantic or nonfluent PPA, in contrast to what has been previously reported. In semantic PPA, evidence pointed to oscillatory speeding, not the slowing that was previously reported in a single-case study. In nonfluent PPA, spectral power fell between logopenic and semantic PPA, suggesting there is oscillatory slowing but to a lesser extent than logopenic PPA. The CNN was relatively successful in distinguishing PPA from healthy controls (F1 = 0.851). The CNN did not perform as well on four-way classification (lvPPA, svPPA, nfvPPA, controls; F1 = 0.586) but was significantly above chance. These results are promising and suggest that resting-state EEG may prove useful as a biomarker for PPA diagnosis. Potential factors underlying the differences between the findings of the current study and previous work are discussed.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In 2011, consensus was reached on the diagnostic criteria for a relatively new neurodegenerative syndrome, primary progressive aphasia (PPA). With the advent of the criteria for PPA diagnosis comes the opportunity to explore potential diagnostic tools for more accurate and earlier detection of PPA and its three variants. Earlier and more accurate detection leads to faster implementation of a treatment plan, which can then be better tailored to suit the needs of the patient and lead to better outcomes. The current study aimed to investigate the potential use of resting-state electroencephalography (EEG) and convolutional neural networks (CNN) as a tool for accurate diagnosis of PPA.

PPA is a relatively rare form of dementia that is primarily characterized by impairments in speech and language that gradually worsen over time. Unlike other types of dementia, in PPA, speech and language deficits are the predominant clinical features at onset and continue to be primary domains of impairment with disease progression. Standard exclusionary criteria for PPA diagnosis include symptoms caused by another neurological or psychiatric condition, prominent memory impairment, prominent visuospatial deficits, global motor impairment, or prominent behavioral disturbances (Gorno-Tempini et al., 2011). Early and accurate diagnosis of PPA can be difficult due to the relative rarity of the condition and subtle symptoms that characterize the earliest stages of disease progression. The insidious nature of symptoms and the early age of onset, generally in the patient's mid-40's to early 60's (Gorno-Tempini et al., 2011; Mesulam et al., 2014), are also important clinical considerations in PPA, necessitating effective tools for detection and diagnosis.

Once a PPA diagnosis is established, the disorder can be further delineated into three variants by the presence or absence of specific speech and language characteristics and further supported by distinct underlying patterns of brain atrophy. The core features of the nonfluent/agrammatic variant (nfvPPA) are apraxia of speech and/or agrammatism due to fronto-insular atrophy that is greater in the language dominant hemisphere. Individuals with nfvPPA must also have at least two of the following: impaired syntax comprehension, spared single word comprehension, and spared object knowledge. Individuals with semantic variant (svPPA) have a loss of core semantic knowledge due to anterior temporal lobe atrophy (greater in the language dominant hemisphere) leading to the presence of both anomia and single-word comprehension deficits. Individuals with svPPA must also have at least three of the following: loss of object knowledge, surface dyslexia/dysgraphia, spared repetition, and spared grammar and motor speech. The logopenic variant (lvPPA) is characterized by both impaired word retrieval and impaired phrase and sentence repetition due to temporo-parietal atrophy that is greater in the language dominant hemisphere. Individuals with lvPPA must also have at least three of the following: phonemic paraphasias, spared single word comprehension and object knowledge, spared motor speech, and lack of frank agrammatism (Gorno-Tempini et al., 2011). Each of these three canonical PPA variants has been linked to distinct neuropathology. Frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD) is the underlying cause of most nfvPPA and svPPA cases whereas Alzheimer's disease has been linked to lvPPA (Spinelli et al., 2017). For nfvPPA and svPPA, the specific proteinopathy of FTLD varies, with FTLD-tau being the primary cause in nfvPPA and FTLD-TDP43 being the primary cause in svPPA. Diagnosis typically requires extensive, time-consuming assessment batteries inclusive of neurological, neuropsychological, and speech and language tests. Further, although there are characteristics that are unique to each clinical variant, there is some overlap across variants, which can introduce complication into differential diagnosis (Ranasinghe et al., 2017).

In recent years, researchers have sought to identify biomarkers to aid in PPA diagnosis using non-invasive functional neuroimaging. Non-invasive neuroimaging methods like EEG, magnetoencephalography (MEG), and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) are typically well tolerated and can be applied in a clinical setting. However, MRI and MEG are costly, and the use of a high-powered magnet makes them unsafe for people with certain contraindications, including medical devices such as pacemakers or cochlear implants, or implanted metal following surgery. Utilizing EEG has certain advantages in that the cost of EEG recording is negligible, and it is safe for use in all individuals.

EEG is a type of non-invasive neuroimaging technique that records the brain's electrical activity via electrodes that are placed on the scalp (Luck, 2014). Data collected via EEG can be either resting-state (i.e., task-free), whereby the participant's behavior is passive and not directed by any external stimulus, or can be task-based, in which the participant is asked to engage in some activity. In EEG (and MEG), researchers can use spectral analysis to examine the frequency content of the neural signal in different frequency bands, including delta (1–4 Hz), theta (4–7 Hz), alpha (8–12 Hz), and beta (12–30 Hz) bands. In individuals with a suspected neurodegenerative disease, resting-state data are easier to obtain and interpret than task-based data, as patients may be unable to complete tasks due to cognitive or other impairments (Dalton et al., 2021). Examination of power spectral density (PSD) in resting-state EEG has potential for use in early detection of neurodegenerative disease due to its high accessibility, relatively low cost, good test-retest reliability, and lack of contraindications (Cecchetti et al., 2024; Dalton et al., 2021; Moral-Rubio et al., 2021). Spectral analysis of resting-state EEG may offer benefit as a tool for detection of PPA, allowing for efficient diagnosis and effective management.

Distinct spectral changes have been observed in individuals with PPA (Kielar et al., 2019, 2022; Ranasinghe et al., 2017; Shah-Basak et al., 2019). Shah-Basak et al. (2019) identified oscillatory slowing in PPA using resting-state MEG, noted by increased power in the delta band and decreased power in the alpha band. Kielar et al. (2019) extended these findings by examining both resting-state and task-based MEG. Findings revealed that individuals with nfvPPA and lvPPA showed slower processing in language-elicited responses using task-based MEG, as evidenced by a significant delay in their peak latencies (Kielar et al., 2019). Additionally, Shah-Basak et al. (2019) observed spectral slowing, evidenced by an increase of resting-state delta and theta power in association with decreases in alpha and beta power (Kielar et al., 2019). These results are notable, as they are distinct from neural oscillations seen in typically aging adults: oscillatory speeding characterized by decreased power in lower frequency bands and increased power in higher frequency bands (Kielar et al., 2019). However, distinguishable differences in PSD between nfvPPA and lvPPA were not observed (Kielar et al., 2019). In svPPA, a single case study utilizing task-based and resting-state MEG revealed slower processing speeds in the language-elicited task-based MEG and decreased power in the higher frequency alpha and beta bands in the resting-state MEG (Kielar et al., 2022). There was no evidence of increased spectral power in the lower-frequency delta and theta bands. While this may seem to contradict previous studies examining PSD in PPA, these results are not entirely incongruous with PSD changes observed in people with FTLD, the underlying pathology for svPPA (and, often, nfvPPA; Spinelli et al., 2017). Previous studies have observed a decrease in spectral power in the alpha and beta frequency bands and no increase in the delta or theta frequency bands in FTLD (Lindau et al., 2003). Overall, these findings indicate that examination of PSD could contribute to PPA diagnosis.

Only a few studies have attempted PPA classification using resting-state EEG or MEG. Ranasinghe and colleagues (2017) used resting-state MEG connectivity in delta, theta, alpha, and beta frequency bands to perform PPA subtype classification via logistic regression. The resting-state MEG findings mirrored the findings of Kielar et al. (2019) and Shah-Basak et al. (2019), revealing a slowing of neural activity, indicated by reduced spectral power in the alpha and beta frequency bands in all three PPA variants (Ranasinghe et al., 2017). Increased spectral power in the delta-theta range was also observed in all three variants of PPA, but was most notable in lvPPA (Ranasinghe et al., 2017). Logistic regression with PPA variant as the outcome measure and MEG connectivity as the predictor was moderately successful in discriminating the three PPA variants, with the best outcomes observed for lvPPA relative to nfvPPA and svPPA.

Of greatest relevance to the current study, two recent studies examined resting-state EEG in relation to PPA diagnosis (Cecchetti et al., 2024; Moral-Rubio et al., 2021). Cecchetti et al. (2024) observed an overall slowing in lvPPA and increased power in the theta band in nfvPPA, consistent with other studies. The most significant finding of the study was the identification of distinct patterns of activity associated with lvPPA. The results aligned with previous studies that demonstrated an overall slowing, as evidenced by increases in the delta and theta bands, and decreases in the alpha band compared to healthy controls, nfvPPA, and svPPA (Cecchetti et al., 2024). Moral-Rubio et al. (2021) utilized graph-theory derived metrics from resting-state EEG combined with machine learning to distinguish PPA from healthy controls and for four-way classification of healthy controls, svPPA, lvPPA, and nfvPPA. Classification of PPA vs. healthy controls was reasonably good (F1 = 0.83), but four-way classification was less successful (F1 = 0.60).

With advances in computational modeling, more sophisticated classification approaches could be useful, such as CNNs, networks that are highly effective at automatically and adaptively learning spatial hierarchies of features from data. Much of past research into EEG analysis with deep learning has been focused on CNNs (Lawhern et al., 2018; Craik et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2019) for the benefits of spatial pattern detection, reduction in trainable parameters as compared to other deep learning models, and modifications that help to prevent overfitting. Outside of the direct classification of the EEG voltage values, CNNs have also been employed to classify spectral features of the EEG signal (Gao et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2020). Classifying spectral features directly has been considered a valid input modification that can help to avoid overfitting to participant-specific EEG amplitude differences or the presence of artifacts. Employing spectral features of EEG signals derived during resting state with the computational power and generalization abilities of CNNs may allow for fast and accurate detection of PPA and potentially the classification of PPA subtype.

The current study sought to contribute to the evidence base evaluating the use of resting-state EEG to distinguish PSD differences in each variant of PPA. This study builds on previous research by using relative PSD in the delta, theta, alpha, and beta frequency bands as input to a CNN for classification of PPA vs. healthy controls and four-way classification of healthy controls, svPPA, lvPPA, and nfvPPA. In addition, in previous research it was not always clear whether resting state data were collected in eyes open or eyes closed conditions. Some research suggests that differences exist in resting state eyes open versus closed conditions, even in the absence of visual input (Boytsova and Danko, 2010). Thus, in the current study, both eyes open and eyes closed resting stage data were collected and reported. Based on the findings in previous research using resting-state EEG, we expected to see a difference in PSD between individuals with PPA and healthy controls within the delta, theta, alpha, and beta frequency bands. We further hypothesized that there would be discernible oscillatory changes in each variant of PPA, facilitating CNN classification. We did not have specific predictions regrading differences between eyes open and eyes closed conditions.

sec.title: Material and methods
sec.p:
2.1. Participants. Data were collected at the Aphasia Research and Treatment Lab at the University of Texas at Austin. Participants with PPA were recruited through a larger clinical trial of speech-language interventions in PPA (NCT04881617). The control participants were either care partners of the PPA participants or were recruited from the Austin, TX community or by word-of-mouth. Thirty-seven participants with PPA (n = 14 lvPPA, n = 11 nfvPPA, n = 12 svPPA) and nine age- and education-matched controls participated in this study. Participants with PPA met diagnostic criteria for PPA and for PPA subtype (Gorno-Tempini et al., 2011) as determined by a comprehensive neurological, neuropsychological, and cognitive-linguistic assessment (Gorno-Tempini et al., 2004; Kramer et al., 2003; Knopman et al., 2008; Staffaroni et al., 2019). This assessment was designed to probe general cognitive-linguistic function (e.g., Mini Mental State Exam) and each of the domains needed for differential diagnosis of the three PPA variants (e.g., single word retrieval [Boston Naming Test], single word comprehension [Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test], syntactic processing [Northwestern Anagram Test, Syntax Comprehension], phonological short-term memory [Western Aphasia Battery-Repetition], apraxia of speech [AOS rating]). Participants with PPA were in the mild to moderate stages of PPA progression. Demographic information and results of neuropsychological testing are presented in Table 1, Table 2. This study was approved by the University of Texas at Austin Institutional Review Board and written informed consent was obtained for all participants. Control participants were compensated $15/hour for their time in the study.Table 1
Demographic information for each participant group.Table 1
HC (n = 9)svPPA (n = 12)lvPPA (n = 14)nfvPPA (n = 11)
Hearing Thresholds (Pure Tone Average)22.2 (12.2)30.1 (19.4)19.1 (7.2)29.3 (7.9)
Sex8F/1M6F/6M9F/5M3F/8M
Age (years)67.4 (6.3)65.6 (8.0)67.9 (8.2)68.6 (8.6)
Education (years)15.6 (2.2)16.4 (3.0)16.2 (2.2)16.1 (2.5)
Notes: Pure Tone Average (air conduction) across both ears at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz, M = Male, F = Female. The mean (standard deviation) for each group is reported.Table 2
Results from cognitive-neuropsychological assessment for each PPA variant. Brackets indicate the total possible score on a test.Table 2

Western Aphasia Battery-Revised Repetition [100]b92.00 (5.03)72.50 (10.53)h89.60 (5.46)i
Pyramid & Palm Trees: Pictures [svPPA/lvPPA: 52; nfvPPA: 14 or 25]c41.83 (6.58)47.67 (2.64)h14.00 (0.00)/24.00 (1.00)i
Boston Naming Test [svPPA/lvPPA: 60; nfvPPA: 30]a13.08 (9.02)33.17 (14.94)h19.00 (5.74)
Syntax Comprehension [100]d98.16 (2.53)g88.52 (8.30)h26.18 (3.84)
NAT [100]e79.70 (26.27)g68.75 (23.06)h90.44 (8.39)
AOS Ratingf0.00 (0.00)0.08 (0.28)h62.12 (18.52)
Notes: MMSE = Mini-Mental State Exam, AOS = Apraxia of Speech (0 = no AOS, 3 = moderate AOS, 7 = severe AOS), NAT = Northwestern Anagram Test. The mean (standard deviation) for each group is reported.a
Assessments from neuropsychological battery described in Knopman et al. (2008); Kramer et al. (2003); Staffaroni et al. (2019). For the Boston Naming Test, the full 60-item version was only administered to individuals with svPPA and lvPPA. Individuals with nfvPPA received a shortened 30-item version.b
From Kertesz and Raven, 2007).c
The full 52-item Pyramids and Palm Trees test (Howard and Patterson, 1992) was only given to individuals with svPPA or lvPPA. Individuals with nfvPPA received a shorter version, developed by Breining et al. (2015) from the standard 52-items version.d
From Wilson et al., 2010). A subset of participants completed an auditory sentence-picture matching task developed by Lukic (2024).e
From Weintraub et al., 2009). The full 30-item version was only administered to individuals with nfvPPA. Individuals with lvPPA and svPPA received a shortened 12-item version. Percent correct is reported.f
Subjective clinician ratings from the Motor Speech Examination (Wertz et al., 1984).g
Of the 12 individuals with svPPA, one did not complete the California Verbal Learning Test, Complex Figure Copy and Recall, or Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test, and one did not complete the syntax comprehension or the NAT.h
Of the 14 individuals with lvPPA, three individuals completed different testing batteries due to changes in the testing protocol over time. One participant did not complete the syntax comprehension or NAT. One participant did not complete the California Verbal Learning Test, Peabody Picture Vocabulary Test, verbal fluency, Western Aphasia Battery, syntax comprehension, or NAT, and did not receive an AOS rating. One participant did not complete the Western Aphasia Battery, verbal fluency, or syntax comprehension. The latter two participants also completed the 30-item version of the Boston Naming Test (correct responses = 22 and 20) and a 25-item version of the Pyramids and Palm Trees Test (correct responses = 24 and 25). The Boston Naming Test and Pyramids and Palm Trees data are not included in the mean (standard deviation) presented in the table above.i
Of the 11 participants with nfvPPA, one participant did not complete the Western Aphasia Battery. Six of the 11 participants completed the 14-item version of the Pyramids and Palm Trees and five completed the 25-item version. Separate means (standard deviations) are presented.

sec.title: EEG acquisition and procedure
sec.p:
EEG data were sampled at 25,000 Hz using a 32-channel active electrode system (BrainVision actiCHamp, Brain Products, Gilching, Germany) with 10–20 placement, the ground electrode at Fpz, and an average mastoid reference (TP9 and TP10). Pre-recorded instructions were presented binaurally using insert earphones (ER- 3A; Etymotic Research, Elk Grove Village, IL) via E-Prime 2.0 software (Schneider et al., 2012). Participants were instructed to sit with their feet flat on the floor and to either keep their eyes open or closed. Eyes open and eyes closed conditions alternated in pseudo-randomized one-minute intervals (closed, open, open, closed, open, closed, closed, open). We recorded eight minutes total of resting-state EEG: four minutes with eyes open and four minutes with eyes closed (note that this is twice as much resting-state EEG data as Dalton et al., 2021). The specific instructions that participants heard were.1.
Intro prompt: “We will now be making some baseline recordings with your eyes open and closed. This will take us about ten minutes”2.
Eyes closed prompt: “Now let's record with your eyes closed. Sitting as you are now, simply close your eyes and relax. I'll record for one minute and then let you know when I'm done. While I'm recording, please sit with your feet flat on the floor and keep your head and body still. Ready?”3.
Eyes open prompt: “Now let's record with your eyes open. Sitting as you are now, I would like you to focus your gaze on a spot of the wall in front of you that you can see comfortably. I'd like you to keep looking at that spot while I record your brainwaves for about one minute. It's okay if you need to blink, however I need you to hold your head and body still while I record. So look at that spot and I'll let you know when I'm done recording. Ready?”4.
Subsequent prompts for eyes open/closed are simplified versions of the above (e.g., “Now let's record again with your eyes closed. Sitting as you are now, simply close your eyes and relax. I'll record for one minute and let you know when I'm done. Ready?”)."

sec.title: EEG preprocessing
sec.p: EEG data were preprocessed using EEGLAB 2019.1 (Delorme and Makeig, 2004) in MATLAB 2021b (MathWorks Inc., Natick, Massachusetts, USA). Preprocessing was blinded by participant. Raw EEG data were resampled to 128 Hz, then filtered from one to 60 Hz using a non-causal, Hamming windowed-sinc FIR filter (high pass filter cut-off = 1 Hz, filter order = 212; low pass filter cut-off = 60 Hz, filter order = 44). Channels with activity greater than three standard deviations from surrounding channels were rejected and replaced via spherical spline interpolation; the mean number of interpolated channels for control participants was 1.4 (range = 0–3), for participants with svPPA was 1.3 (range = 0–3), for participants with nfvPPA was 1.8 (range = 0–3), and for participants with lvPPA was 2.0 (range = 0–3). Artifact subspace reconstruction (ASR) was subsequently used to suppress large artifacts (Mullen et al., 2015). Approximately 60 seconds of clean data were manually identified and input as the calibration data for ASR. Lastly, independent component analysis (ICA) was performed to correct eye-movement, muscle, and electrocardiographic artifacts. ICA was performed using the infomax algorithm runica, adjusted to extract 25 components in order to meet full rank order assumptions of ICA (Li et al., 2020). Components were removed based on manual inspection of the time-course, topography, and power spectra of the component. The mean number of components removed for control participants was 5.2 (range = 3–8), for participants with svPPA was 6.7 (range = 0–13), for participants with nfvPPA was 5.2 (range = 2–10), and for participants with lvPPA was 5.4 (range = 3–8).

From the preprocessed data, PSD was computed using the pmtm function in MATLAB 2021b with a time-half bandwidth product of 4. Relative power was then extracted for the delta (1–4 Hz), theta (4–8 Hz), alpha (8–12 Hz), and beta (12–30 Hz) bands for further analysis. Of note, several previous studies examined combined delta and theta bands rather than evaluating delta and theta separately. Thus, as a complement to our separate delta and theta analyses, we also ran analyses with a combined delta-theta band. The results for the combined delta-theta band are presented in Supplementary Materials.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: PSD data were analyzed using linear mixed effects modeling (LMEM) in R (version 4.1.2) using the lme4 package (Bates et al., 2015). We included relative PSD as the outcome measure and group (controls, svPPA, lvPPA, nfvPPA) as the fixed effect. We included participant as a random intercept. Separate models were run for each electrode channel for each frequency band of interest and for eyes open and eyes closed conditions (e.g., Channel 1 delta band eyes open, Channel 1 delta band eyes closed, Channel 2 delta band eyes open, etc.). Data met assumptions for the use of LMEM. Electrode clusters were subsequently identified based on the magnitude of the F-statistic and spatial proximity using the spatial_neighbors and find_clusters functions (retrieved from http://kutaslab.ucsd.edu/matlabmk_fn_docs/index.html). Only electrodes where p < 0.05 were considered for inclusion in a cluster and only clusters containing more than one electrode are reported. LMEMs were then run for each cluster and pairwise post hoc analyses were conducted to identify differences across groups. The emmeans package (Lenth et al., 2019) was used to conduct these pairwise comparisons with Tukey's family-wise correction for multiple comparisons.

sec.title: CNN architectures and leave-one-out training
sec.p: To assess the capability of a deep learning network to distinguish between classifications in both four-class (control, nfvPPA, svPPA, lvPPA) and two-class (control, PPA) problems, a leave-one-out (LOO) training approach was designed. Within this framework, data from a single participant were excluded from the training process, enabling the training of the model with the data from the remaining participants. Subsequently, the trained model predicted the classification of the left-out participant. This procedure was iterated for each participant, and the performance metrics for both the two-class and four-class categorizations were then reported. LOO training was selected rather than a standard train/validate/test split, as the latter may lead to higher or lower performances depending on the random selection of participants for each set.

A custom CNN was developed to differentiate among participant PSD curves derived from EEG data recorded during resting state.1 In computing PSDs, we assessed the impact of varying EEG sample lengths (one, two, and 4 seconds) on the consistency of PSD estimates, observing that longer samples led to reduced within-participant variability. PSDs were calculated using the Welch method at a resolution of 1 Hz, producing data of size (32, 64) for each PSD sample. The gamma band was then removed by keeping only the first 30 frequency bins. Given the considerable variability in channel-wise PSDs and the limitations imposed by our dataset size, we opted for channel-averaged PSDs, thereby condensing the data to a size of (1, 30).

Our exploration into CNN architecture was aimed towards minimizing the number of trainable parameters and implementing design choices to curtail overfitting. Each assessed CNN was constructed with a varied number of convolutional blocks. Specifically, each convolutional block consisted of a 1D convolution layer, average pooling, batch normalization, ReLU (rectified linear unit) activation, and dropout. Both dropout and batch normalization have been used successfully to prevent overfitting. Through employing 1 × 2 average pooling post-convolution and dense concatenation connections between each block, the receptive field of the network across PSD frequency bins was progressively broadened. Following the feature extraction architecture section facilitated by the convolutional blocks, each evaluated architecture incorporated two dense layers before the classification layer, further reducing the number of trainable parameters. The final architecture is presented in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
CNN architecture employed for classification. The only modification when comparing the two- and four-class formulations was in the final dense classification layer. CNN = convolutional neural network, ReLU = rectified linear unit activation.Fig. 1

The limited optimization of the aforementioned CNN architecture was accomplished by comparing performance outcomes across the LOO training. For each participant, all available resting-state EEG data were segmented into samples corresponding to the selected duration for each PSD calculation. The LOO CNN model was trained using the data from all other participants and then evaluated on the data from the left-out participant. The allocation of participant data into training and validation sets (60% for training and 40% for validation) was chosen to improve model generalization to novel participant data. Meticulous measures were undertaken to avert potential data leakage during the formation of LOO datasets. The determination of each participant's decoded class was based on the majority class among all decoded PSD samples. Training was executed utilizing the ADAM optimizer with a learning rate of 0.005, categorical cross-entropy loss, and a batch size of 32.

sec.title: Power spectral density (PSD) analyses
sec.p: Spectral power plots for each participant group for a subset of electrodes are presented in Supplementary Figs. 8–16. Each plot presents the mean for each group along with the individual participant data, providing information about individual participant variability. Note that these plots do not distinguish eyes open and eyes closed conditions but are intended to provide a representative view of the spectral power for each group.

sec.title: Cluster identification
sec.p:
The results of the LMEM for each frequency band and condition (eyes open, eyes closed) are presented in Fig. 2 (delta-theta is presented in Supplementary Fig. 1). Clusters were identified based on these data (i.e., the F-statistic and spatial proximity). Separate clusters were identified for eyes open and eyes closed conditions, although substantial overlap was observed across conditions. The clusters are presented in Supplementary Figs. 2–4. Data from these clusters were used for subsequent analyses.Fig. 2
Topoplots of the F-statistics derived from the linear mixed effects models with power spectral density as the outcome measure and group as the predictor. Panel A is delta band, panel B is theta band, panel C is alpha band, and panel D is beta band. Results presented separately for the eyes open and eyes closed conditions.Fig. 2

sec.title: Delta
sec.p:
Clusters 1–3 are presented in Supplementary Fig. 1. Significant differences were observed between controls and lvPPA in cluster 2 (beta = −0.090, SE = 0.032, t (42) = −2.844, p = 0.033), between lvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1 and 2 (Cluster 1: beta = 0.144, SE = 0.0328, t (42) = 4.378, p < 0.001; Cluster 2: beta = 0.096, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 3.297, p = 0.010), and between lvPPA and nfvPPA in cluster 3 (beta = 0.078, SE = 0.290, t (42) = 2.708, p = 0.046). In all instances, this reflected a greater relative PSD in the delta band for lvPPA (Fig. 3, Table 3). No other significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the delta band, eyes open condition.Fig. 3
Relative power spectral density in the delta band eyes open condition for clusters 1–3.Fig. 3Table 3
Relative power spectral density in the delta band eyes open condition for clusters 1–3.Table 3
MeanStandard Error95% Confidence Interval
Cluster 1
Control0.2310.0280.174–0.287
lvPPA0.3210.0220.276–0.366
nfvPPA0.2370.0250.187–0.288
svPPA0.1780.0240.129–0.226
Cluster 2
Control0.1630.0250.113–0.213
lvPPA0.2530.0200.213–0.293
nfvPPA0.1790.0220.134–0.225
svPPA0.1570.0210.114–0.200
Cluster 3
Control0.1460.0240.097–0.194
lvPPA0.2270.0190.188–0.266
nfvPPA0.1490.0220.105–0.192
svPPA0.1600.0210.118–0.201

sec.title: Theta
sec.p:
Clusters 1–4 are presented in Supplementary Fig. 2. Significant differences were observed between lvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1, 2, 3, and 4 (Cluster 1: beta = 0.108, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 3.769, p = 0.0027; Cluster 2: beta = 0.112, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 3.888, p = 0.002; Cluster 3: beta = 0.116, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 4.052, p = 0.001; Cluster 4: beta = 0.090, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 3.070, p = 0.019), and between HC and lvPPA in cluster 3 (beta = −0.100, SE = 0.031, t (42) = −3.206, p = 0.013). This reflects increased relative PSD in the theta band for lvPPA compared to HC and svPPA (Fig. 4, Table 4). No other significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the theta band, eyes open condition.Fig. 4
Relative power spectral density in the theta band eyes open condition for clusters 1–4.Fig. 4Table 4
Relative power spectral density in the theta band eyes open condition for clusters 1–4.Table 4
MeanStandard Error95% Confidence Interval
Cluster 1
Control0.2170.0240.168–0.266
lvPPA0.2680.0190.229–0.307
nfvPPA0.2000.0220.156–0.245
svPPA0.1610.0210.118–0.203
Cluster 2
Control0.1850.0240.136–0.234
lvPPA0.2550.0200.215–0.294
nfvPPA0.1840.0220.139–0.228
svPPA0.1430.0210.100–0.186
Cluster 3
Control0.1640.0240.115–0.213
lvPPA0.2640.0200.225–0.304
nfvPPA0.1950.0220.151–0.240
svPPA0.1480.0210.105–0.190
Cluster 4
Control0.2080.0250.158–0.258
lvPPA0.2600.0200.220–0.300
nfvPPA0.2070.0220.161–0.252
svPPA0.1710.0210.128–0.214

sec.title: Alpha
sec.p: No significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the alpha band, eyes open condition.

sec.title: Beta
sec.p:
Clusters 1–4 are presented in Supplementary Fig. 3. Significant differences were observed between HC and lvPPA in cluster 2 (beta = 0.126, SE = 0.035, t (42) = 3.639, p = 0.004), between lvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1, 2, 3, and 4 (Cluster 1: beta = −0.168, SE = 0.042, t (42) = −4.036, p = 0.001; Cluster 2: beta = −0.115, SE = 0.0318, t (42) = −3.618, p = 0.004; Cluster 3: beta = −0.112, SE = 0.034, t (42) = −3.305, p = 0.010; Cluster 4: beta = −0,111 SE = 0.030, t (42) = −3.720, p = 0.003), and between nfvPPA and svPPA in cluster 4 (beta = −0.093 SE = 0.032, t (42) = −2.951, p = 0.026). In cluster 1, there was a trend toward increased beta power in svPPA relative to HC and nfvPPA, though this did not survive correction for multiple comparisons (ps = 0.080 and 0.081, respectively). Overall, the findings reflected a greater relative PSD in the beta band for HC and svPPA relative to lvPPA and nfvPPA (Fig. 5, Table 5). No other significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the beta band, eyes open condition.Fig. 5
Relative power spectral density in the beta band eyes open condition for clusters 1–4.Fig. 5Table 5
Relative power spectral density in the beta band eyes open condition for clusters 1–4.Table 5
MeanStandard Error95% Confidence Interval
Cluster 1
Control0.3330.0350.262–0.405
lvPPA0.2800.0280.223–0.338
nfvPPA0.3400.0320.275–0.404
svPPA0.4490.0310.387–0.511
Cluster 2
Control0.4050.0270.350–0.459
lvPPA0.2790.0220.235–0.323
nfvPPA0.3240.0240.275–0.373
svPPA0.3940.0230.347–0.441
Cluster 3
Control0.4080.0290.350–0.466
lvPPA0.3130.0230.266–0.359
nfvPPA0.3450.0260.292–0.397
svPPA0.4250.0250.374–0.475
Cluster 4
Control0.3660.0250.316–0.417
lvPPA0.3040.0200.264–0.345
nfvPPA0.3220.0230.276–0.368
svPPA0.4150.0220.371–0.459

sec.title: Delta
sec.p:
Clusters 1–4 are presented in Supplementary Fig. 4. Significant differences were observed between HC and lvPPA in clusters 2, 3, and 4 (Cluster 2: beta = −0.096, SE = 0.032, t (42) = −3.030, p = 0.021; Cluster 3: beta = −0.102, SE = 0.030, t (42) = −3.384, p = 0.008; Cluster 4: beta = −0.088, SE = 0.028, t (42) = −3.172 p = 0.015), between lvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1, 2, 3, and 4 (Cluster 1: beta = 0.131, SE = 0.034, t (42) = 3.884, p = 0.002; Cluster 2: beta = 0.105, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 3.594, p = 0.005; Cluster 3: beta = 0.088, SE = 0.028, t (42) = 3.165, p = 0.015; Cluster 4: beta = 0.083, SE = 0.025, t (42) = 3.279, p = 0.011), and between lvPPA and nfvPPA in clusters 1 and 3 (Cluster 1: beta = 0.095, SE = 0.035, t (42) = 2.761, p = 0.041; Cluster 3: beta = 0.087, SE = 0.029, t (42) = 3.057, p = 0.020). In all instances, this reflected a greater relative PSD in the delta band for lvPPA (Fig. 6, Table 6). No other significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the delta band, eyes closed condition.Fig. 6
Relative power spectral density in the delta band eyes closed condition for clusters 1–4.Fig. 6Table 6
Relative power spectral density in the delta band eyes closed condition for clusters 1–4.Table 6
MeanStandard Error95% Confidence Interval
Cluster 1
Control0.2110.0290.153–0.268
lvPPA0.3030.0230.257–0.349
nfvPPA0.2080.0260.156–0.260
svPPA0.1720.0250.122–0.222
Cluster 2
Control0.1450.0250.0948–0.195
lvPPA0.2410.0200.201–0.282
nfvPPA0.1630.0230.117–0.208
svPPA0.1360.0220.093–0.180
Cluster 3
Control0.1140.0240.067–0.162
lvPPA0.2160.0190.178–0.254
nfvPPA0.1290.0210.086–0.172

Cluster 4

lvPPA0.2090.0170.174–0.244
nfvPPA0.1500.0200.111–0.189
svPPA0.1260.0190.088–0.163

sec.title: Theta
sec.p:
Clusters 1–3 are presented in Supplementary Fig. 5. Significant differences were observed between HC and lvPPA in cluster 2 (beta = −0.116, SE = 0.034, t (42) = −3.432, p = 0.007) and between lvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1, 2, and 3 (Cluster 1: beta = 0.110, SE = 0.032, t (42) = 3.510 p = 0.006; Cluster 2: beta = 0.131, SE = 0.031, t (42) = 4.217, p = 0.001; Cluster 3: beta = 0.097, SE = 0.033, t (42) = 2.978, p = 0.024). This reflects increased relative PSD in the theta band for lvPPA compared to HC and svPPA (Fig. 7, Table 7). No other significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the theta band, eyes closed condition.Fig. 7
Relative power spectral density in the theta band eyes closed condition for clusters 1–3.Fig. 7Table 7
Relative power spectral density in the theta band eyes closed condition for clusters 1–3.Table 7
MeanStandard Error95% Confidence Interval
Cluster 1
Control0.2020.0270.148–0.256
lvPPA0.2680.0210.225–0.311
nfvPPA0.1950.0240.146–0.244
svPPA0.1580.0230.111–0.204
Cluster 2
Control0.1610.0260.108–0.214
lvPPA0.2770.0210.234–0.319
nfvPPA0.1920.0240.144–0.240
svPPA0.1460.0230.100–0.192
Cluster 3
Control0.2020.0280.147–0.258
lvPPA0.2680.0220.224–0.313
nfvPPA0.2010.0250.150–0.251
svPPA0.1710.0240.123–0.220

sec.title: Alpha
sec.p: No significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the alpha band, eyes closed condition.

sec.title: Beta
sec.p:
Clusters 1–3 are presented in Supplementary Fig. 6. Significant differences were observed between nfvPPA in cluster 2 (beta = 0.096, SE = 0.034 t (42) = 2.866, p = 0.032), between lvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1 and 3 (Cluster 1: beta = −0.103, SE = 0.034 t (42) = −3.076, p = 0.019; Cluster 3: beta = −0.103, SE = 0.037 t (42) = −2.764, p = 0.041), and between nfvPPA and svPPA in clusters 1 and 2 (Cluster 1: beta = −0.097, SE = 0.036 t (42) = −2.728, p = 0.044; Cluster 2: beta = −0.090, SE = 0.031 t (42) = −2.903, p = 0.029), This reflects reduced relative PSD in the beta band for nfvPPA compared to HC and svPPA, and between lvPPA and svPPA (Fig. 8, Table 8). No other significant differences were observed for relative PSD in the beta band, eyes closed condition.Fig. 8
Relative power spectral density in the beta band eyes closed condition for clusters 1–3.Fig. 8Table 8
Relative power spectral density in the beta band eyes closed condition for clusters 1–3.Table 8
MeanStandard Error95% Confidence Interval
Cluster 1
Control0.3260.0280.269–0.383
lvPPA0.2360.0230.190–0.282
nfvPPA0.2420.0260.191–0.294
svPPA0.3400.0250.290–0.389
Cluster 2
Control0.3570.0250.307–0.407
lvPPA0.2730.0200.233–0.314
nfvPPA0.2610.0230.216–0.306
svPPA0.3510.0220.308–0.395
Cluster 3
Control0.2790.0320.216–0.343
lvPPA0.2490.0250.198–0.300
nfvPPA0.2480.0290.191–0.306
svPPA0.3520.0270.297–0.407

sec.title: Summary
sec.p: The results were largely consistent across the eyes open and eyes closed conditions. Within each frequency band, similar, though not identical, clusters were identified between eyes open and eyes closed conditions (see Supplementary Figs. 2–5), and all findings were in the same direction. Consistent significant findings were observed across conditions in the: delta band, where individuals with lvPPA had greater PSD than controls and individuals with svPPA and nfvPPA; theta band, where individuals with lvPPA had greater PSD than controls and individuals with svPPA; alpha band, where no significant differences were observed in either condition; and beta band, where individuals with lvPPA had reduced PSD relative to individuals with svPPA and individuals with svPPA had increased PSD relative to individuals with nfvPPA. In the eyes open condition only, individuals with lvPPA had significantly decreased beta relative to the control group. In the eyes closed condition only, significantly decreased beta power was observed for individuals with nfvPPA relative to healthy controls. Taken together, unique patterns of PSD emerged across frequency bands in each PPA variant. Most importantly, this motivated the second part of the study, where relative PSD was used as input to a CNN developed to classify participants by PPA variant.

sec.title: CNN classification of PPA
sec.p:
The general CNN architecture and input formulation derived from the limited LOO optimization resulted in a 4-s derived PSD input, and an architecture with three convolutional blocks, each linked with the previous block's feature vector through dense concatenation connections, followed by two dense layers comprising 32 and 8 hidden units, respectively (Fig. 1). The classification of each participant was found based on a majority of classified PSD samples. The resulting recall, precision, weighted F1, and overall accuracy are reported in Table 9 and confusion matrices are presented in Fig. 9.Table 9
Summary of the performances obtained through the LOO training for both the four- and two-class problem formulations.Table 9
Subtype Classification
ClassRecallPrecisionF1Weighted F1AccuracyWeighted AccuracyChance AccuracyWeighted Chance Accuracy
Control0.6670.6000.6320.58666.7%58.7%19.6%25.6%
lvPPA0.7140.7140.71471.4%30.4%
nfvPPA0.4550.5560.50045.5%23.9%
svPPA0.5000.4620.48050.0%26.1%
Presence of Aphasia
ClassRecallPrecisionF1Weighted F1AccuracyWeighted AccuracyChance AccuracyWeighted Chance Accuracy
Control0.6670.6000.6320.85166.7%84.8%19.6%68.5%
PPA0.8920.9170.91789.2%80.4%Fig. 9
Resulting confusion matrices for the four- and two-class PPA classification. Each value in the confusion matrix represents the number of participants classified with that True and Predicted class label, with the diagonal blue squares representing correctly classified participants. The confusion matrices in the bottom panel also present model performances in a per-class perspective. The blue percentages on the bottom of each confusion matrix represent the portion of predictions of each class that were correct, whereas the blue percentages to the right of each confusion matrix represent the portion of participants that the model correctly predicted.Fig. 9

The four-class results in Table 9 and Fig. 9 show that, whereas classification of the control and lvPPA classes was moderately effective, the CNN model had trouble differentiating nfvPPA and svPPA. Whereas the model classified more participants with these variants as the correct variant than any other individual variant, further optimization of the model and/or feature inclusion methods may improve upon these results. The promising lvPPA classification and the two-class presence of progressive aphasia results provide support that deep learning and PSDs can be used for PPA detection as well as classification, particularly for individuals with lvPPA.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Diagnosis of primary progressive aphasia (PPA) presents challenges due to the insidious nature of disease progression and subtle and overlapping clinical features in PPA variants. Extensive neurological, neuropsychological, and speech-language assessments are typically used to arrive at an accurate diagnosis, often leading to fatigue and frustration for the individuals undergoing testing (Gallée et al., 2023). Previous studies indicate that shifts in EEG power spectral density (PSD) could serve as a preclinical assessment to identify neurodegenerative syndromes before clinical criteria are met (Rossini et al., 2007). In PPA, changes in PSD have been observed, primarily reflecting an overall slowing of neural oscillations, characterized by reduced relative PSD in the higher frequency bands, alpha and beta, and increased relative PSD in the lower frequency bands, delta and theta (Cecchetti et al., 2024; Kielar et al., 2019; Kielar et al., 2022; Ranasinghe et al., 2017; Shah-Basak et al., 2019). In the current study, we built on previous research by investigating PSD as a potential PPA biomarker across all three PPA variants. Specifically, we analyzed relative PSD in the delta, theta, delta-theta (supplementary materials), alpha, and beta frequency bands in eyes open and eyes closed resting-state conditions. Additionally, we explored the utility of these data for automated classification of PPA using a convolutional neural network (CNN).

We observed distinct patterns of relative PSD across the three PPA variants. In lvPPA, our findings were in line with previous research: we observed a significant increase in relative PSD in the delta and theta bands and a significant reduction in the beta band, consistent with the hypothesis of oscillatory slowing (Cecchetti et al., 2024; Kielar et al., 2019). This pattern was observed across the brain, in left frontal, left temporo-parietal, left posterior, posterior central, right posterior, and right frontal electrode clusters. The finding of oscillatory slowing is also in line with findings in Alzheimer's dementia (Lindau et al., 2003), which, like lvPPA, is typically caused by underlying Alzheimer's disease pathology (Spinelli et al., 2017; Kielar et al., 2019). In contrast to previous findings, we did not observe the same decrease in power for lvPPA in the alpha band. This may reflect differences in disease severity across the two samples, as the current sample tended to be relatively mild. Notably, three out of six participants with lvPPA in Kielar et al. (2019) had Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA; Nasreddine et al., 2005) scores of 18 or lower, indicating moderate cognitive impairment. Of relevance, previous work in Alzheimer's dementia suggests that oscillatory slowing worsens with disease progression (Cassani et al., 2018).

For svPPA, our findings partially converged with previous research. We observed increased power in the beta band relative to participants with lvPPA and nfvPPA in the eyes open and eyes closed conditions. This was observed across the brain, in left frontal, left temporo-parietal, left posterior parietal, central, right temporo-parietal, and right frontal electrode clusters. PSD for individuals with svPPA was similar to the control participants, with the only difference being a trend toward increased PSD in the beta band in a left frontal cluster in the eyes open condition. As in the current study, Cecchetti et al. (2024) found that participants with svPPA did not differ significantly in PSD from a control group. However, they did not differ from individuals with lvPPA or nfvPPA either, unlike in the current study. In contrast to the single case study by Kielar et al. (2022), we did not observe the same reduction of PSD in the higher frequency bands, although we note that the participant in Kielar et al. was significantly more advanced than participants in the current study (e.g., the participant had a score of 6 on the MoCA). The finding in the current study is also partially consistent with some previous findings in frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD), which is likely to be the underlying pathology in svPPA (Spinelli et al., 2017). Lindau et al. (2003) observed a decrease in PSD in the alpha and beta frequency bands (inconsistent with current findings) with no increase in the delta or theta frequency bands (consistent with current findings). In contrast, Pijnenburg et al. (2008) found that PSD was normal in FTLD. It may be worth noting that Lindau et al. (2003) reported greater slowing in Alzheimer's versus FTLD relative to healthy controls, which is consistent with the findings in the current study. The slight trend toward speeding in svPPA may signify a unique feature of svPPA and should be considered in future research. We also note, however, that the findings are more consistent with what is seen in typical aging (oscillatory speeding) and FTLD than previous work in svPPA.

For nfvPPA, the results indicate an intermediate pattern between lvPPA and svPPA. In the delta and theta bands, the degree of difference between lvPPA and nfvPPA was smaller than that between lvPPA and svPPA in both eyes open and eyes closed conditions. Differences between lvPPA and nfvPPA were observed primarily in left frontal and central electrode clusters. In the beta band, individuals with nfvPPA also displayed a pattern that was more similar to lvPPA, with a smaller difference observed between nfvPPA and lvPPA than between nfvPPA and svPPA. Differences between nfvPPA and svPPA were primarily observed in left posterior parietal, central, and right frontal electrode clusters.

In sum, the primary findings for the PSD analyses were the replication of oscillatory slowing in lvPPA and the novel finding of oscillatory speeding in svPPA (similar to controls in the current sample). In nfvPPA, which is primarily associated with FTLD but may also be caused by other conditions (e.g., Parkinson's disease), the findings fell somewhere between lvPPA and svPPA. Taken together, this lends some support to the hypothesis that changes in oscillatory dynamics may be best explained in relation to the underlying neurodegenerative disease rather than to specific PPA variants (e.g., Cassani et al., 2018; Goossens et al., 2016; Nishida et al., 2011; Ranasinghe et al., 2017). Considering that, for the most part, the three canonical PPA variants are caused by different underlying pathologies (Spinelli et al., 2017), the findings in the current study still suggest that there is potential value in using resting-state EEG PSD for PPA diagnosis.

The findings from the CNN provide preliminary evidence that deep learning classification using PSD as input can help to inform diagnosis of PPA. The CNN performed best on the two-class classification of PPA vs. control (F1 = 0.851) but was still above chance for four-way classification (F1 = 0.586). Four-way classification revealed better classification for controls and individuals with lvPPA, with relatively less effective classification for svPPA and nfvPPA. Compared to Moral-Rubio et al. (2021), which is similar in scope to the current study, the confusion matrices presented in the current study are substantially better, without requiring the extraction of graph-theoretical measures or data reduction techniques that were employed by Moral-Rubio et al. We note, however, that in Moral-Rubio et al., the authors chose a train/test split, rather than a leave-one-out training approach, so the results are not directly comparable. Overall, this study provides preliminary evidence regarding the utility of resting-state EEG PSD as a PPA biomarker.

sec.title: Differences across eyes open and eyes closed conditions
sec.p: In previous research using resting-state EEG in PPA, it has rarely been specified whether the resting-state EEG was collected with eyes open or eyes closed. It has been suggested that differences exist between eyes open and eyes closed resting-state and that these differences are not simply due to the lack of visual input in the eyes closed condition, as differences have been observed even when comparing eyes closed to eyes open in a dark room (Boytsova and Danko, 2010). In the current study, subtle differences were observed across the eyes open and eyes closed conditions, with differences primarily reflecting minor variations in the electrode clusters or in magnitude. The direction of the effects did not change across the two conditions. The differences in localization and magnitude may explain some of the discrepancies observed in the current study relative to previous resting-state research in PPA, although no obvious pattern emerged regarding the clustering of specific electrodes across conditions. Due to the absence of prior information about the nature of the resting-state condition, further examination of this hypothesis is not possible at this time. Given that greater test-retest reliability has been observed in the eyes closed condition (Dalton et al., 2021), it may be the case that the findings from the eyes closed condition in the current study are the most reliable. Future work should investigate the reliability of resting-state EEG in PPA specifically, particularly for eyes-open and eyes-closed conditions.

sec.title: Limitations and future directions
sec.p: As with much research in aphasia and particularly in PPA, the main limitation of the current study is the relatively small sample size (with an unequal number of participants per group). Future studies can be improved by including a greater number of PPA participants and a more equally distributed PPA sample across variants. Furthermore, investigating PPA classification relative to related disorders, such as Alzheimer's dementia, would strengthen the evaluation of the CNN's discriminative abilities. Relatedly, a comparison of each PPA variant to other forms of dementia that share pathology (e.g., Alzheimer's dementia and lvPPA) would help to discern whether the findings in the current study relate primarily to specific syndromes/patterns of brain atrophy vs. underlying disease. If the patterns are indeed better explained by underlying disease, it will also be critical to determine whether there are differences in PSD between FTLD-tau and FTLD-TDP43 proteinopathies, especially considering that both svPPA and nfvPPA have been linked to FTLD. Additionally, exploring the relationship between resting-state EEG and behavioral language measures could deepen our understanding of the deficits observed in PPA and their neural underpinnings. Finally, in the current study, the cognitive-neuropsychological evaluation indicated that all PPA participants were in mild-moderate stages of severity and clearly classifiable as one of the three canonical PPA variants. The longer-term goal of this line of research is early detection and classification of PPA and thus, an important future direction is to examine PSD in a longitudinal design from preclinical to more advanced stages. Of particular relevance, extending investigations to include prodromal PPA cases will bolster the evidence supporting resting-state EEG PSD as a potential PPA biomarker in early disease stages. It is also worth noting that the approach used in the current study may only be useful for canonical PPA variants, whereas up to 40% of PPA cases may be unclassifiable (Sajjadi et al., 2012; although estimates typically are closer to 20%, Utianski et al., 2019). That said, if the findings of the current study indeed extend to prodromal PPA, it would be worth considering whether resting-state EEG could be incorporated into standard clinical assessment and screening for neurodegenerative conditions.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Previous studies suggest that shifts in PSD could be an indicator of neurodegeneration and may be useful as a preclinical assessment before a disorder becomes severe enough to meet clinical criteria (Rossini et al., 2007). Resting-state EEG is a particularly promising technique for examining PSD, as it makes neuroimaging available to patients that are precluded from other widely used forms of imaging and could be better tolerated by some patients. In the current study, unique PSD patterns were observed across three PPA variants and CNN findings suggest that PSD holds promise for PPA classification. Future work should focus on extending these findings to prodromal PPA to determine whether the patterns observed in this study will extend to the preclinical stage. Overall, this study represents a positive step toward utilizing resting-state EEG as a potential PPA biomarker.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Christina Quinn: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Alex Craik: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rachel Tessmer: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Maya L. Henry: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Heather Dial: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

sec.title: TOP guidelines statement
sec.p: The conditions of our ethics approval do not permit public archiving or sharing of the EEG data supporting this study with any individual outside the author team without an institutionally approved data use agreement. Please contact the corresponding author if you are interested.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This was supported by the National Institutes of Health (NIDCD F32DC016812, awarded to Heather Dial; NIDCD R03DC013403 and R01DC016291, awarded to Maya L. Henry).

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: There are no competing interests to disclose.

Investigation of resting-state EEG functional connectivity in frontotemporal lobar degeneration
Distinct spatiotemporal patterns of neuronal functional connectivity in primary progressive aphasia variants
Clinical neurophysiology of aging brain: from normal aging to neurodegeneration
Deep learning-based electroencephalography analysis: a systematic review
Primary progressive aphasia: a tale of two syndromes and the rest
Spontaneous oscillatory markers of cognitive status in two forms of dementia
Typical and atypical pathology in primary progressive aphasia variants
Longitudinal multimodal imaging and clinical endpoints for frontotemporal dementia clinical trials
Clinical and neuroimaging characteristics of clinically unclassifiable primary progressive aphasia

element-citation.source: Am. J. Alzheimer's Dis. Other Dementias

element-citation.part-title: Apraxia Of Speech in Adults: the Disorder and its Management

element-citation.article-title: Connected speech production in three variants of primary progressive aphasia

sec.label: Appendix A
sec.title: Supplementary data
sec.p: The following is the Supplementary data to this article.Multimedia component 1Multimedia component 1

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data sharing requires a Data Use Agreement.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This material is based upon work done as part of the Advanced Fellowship in Geriatrics supported by the US Department of Veterans Affairs Office of Academic Affiliations, the VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System, and the Department of Veterans Affairs Pittsburgh Geriatric Research, Education, and Clinical Center. The contents do not represent the views of the Department of Veterans Affairs or the United States Government. We want to thank the members of the Aphasia Research and Treatment Lab who contributed to participant assessment and care. We also wish to thank G. Nike Gnanateja for sharing his expertise and providing training on EEG data analysis methods. Most importantly, we sincerely thank our participants and their loved ones for their hard work and dedication.

fn.label: Appendix A
fn.p: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ynirp.2025.100242.

Attempts to decode PPA variants directly from raw EEG voltage data and through conversion to covariance matrices were explored but yielded inferior performance when compared to PSD-based input formulations.
